items.title: Zmanim Documentation
items.text:
This site explains common zmanim in plain language. Each page describes what a zman represents and, where available, the basic approach used to calculate it.

You may have reached this site because a zmanim app you use linked you here for more detail about a particular time. These pages are meant to help you understand the time shown in your app, including any sources, offsets, or calculation methods that may affect it.

Note that different apps may use different versions, settings, or defaults, so the information here should be used as a guide rather than a guarantee that every app calculates a zman in exactly the same way.

items.title: Alos 120 Minutes
items.text:
This is deprecated as a caution because it is a very early time. It should be used only lechumra, such as stopping eating at this time on a fast day; using it leniently can result in doing mitzvos hayom too early according to most opinions.

Alos, dawn, calculated very early: 120 minutes before sunrise. It is intended only lechumra, such as stopping eating from this time on a fast day, and not as the start time for mitzvos that can only be performed during the day.

Calculated as 120 fixed minutes before sunrise, or before sea-level sunrise when the elevation setting uses sea level. The 120 minutes are based on the time to walk 5 mil at 24 minutes per mil, following the view that the neshef, the time between dawn and sunrise, does not vary by season or location but depends on the time needed to walk 5 mil.

The time depends on the elevation setting: it is counted back from sunrise or from sea-level sunrise.

This time may not be available where sunrise or sunset cannot be calculated, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getAlos120Minutes

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getAlos120Minutes

items.title: Alos 120 Zmanis
items.text:
This is deprecated as a caution because it is a very early time. It should be used only lechumra, such as stopping eating at this time on a fast day; using it leniently can result in doing mitzvos hayom too early according to most opinions.

Alos, dawn, calculated very early using 120 zmaniyos minutes, meaning ⅙ of the day before sunrise. It is intended only lechumra, such as stopping eating from this time on a fast day, and not as the start time for mitzvos that can only be performed during the day.

Calculated as two GRA shaos zmaniyos before sunrise. This is based on a 24-minute mil, so 5 mil equal 120 minutes, or ⅙ of a 12-hour day. The day is measured from sea-level sunrise to sea-level sunset, or from sunrise to sunset when the elevation setting uses elevation.

The time depends on the elevation setting: the day is based either on sea-level sunrise and sea-level sunset, or on sunrise and sunset using elevation.

This time may not be available where sunrise or sunset cannot be calculated, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getAlos120Zmanis

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getAlos120Zmanis

items.text:
Alos, dawn, when the sun is 18° below the eastern geometric horizon before sunrise.

Calculated by the sun’s position: 18° below the eastern geometric horizon before sunrise.

This time may not be available in northern or southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle or north of the Antarctic Circle, where the sun may not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

items.title: Alos 19 Degrees
items.text:
Alos, dawn, when the sun is 19° below the eastern geometric horizon before sunrise. This is the Rambam’s alos according to Rabbi Moshe Kosower’s Maaglei Tzedek, Ayeles Hashachar Vol. I, page 12, Yom Valayla Shel Torah, Ch. 34, p. 222, and Rabbi Yaakov Shakow’s Luach Ikvei Hayom.

Calculated by the sun’s position: 19° below the eastern geometric horizon before sunrise.

This time may not be available in northern or southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle or north of the Antarctic Circle, where the sun may not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getAlos19Degrees

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getAlos19Degrees

items.title: Alos 19 Point 8 Degrees
items.text:
Alos, dawn, when the sun is 19.8° below the eastern geometric horizon before sunrise. It is a degree-based version of the 90-minutes-before-sunrise alos.

Calculated by the sun’s position: 19.8° below the eastern geometric horizon before sunrise. This degree is based on the sun’s position 90 minutes before sunrise in Jerusalem around the equinox / equilux, which calculates to 19.8° below geometric zenith.

This time may not be available in northern or southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle or north of the Antarctic Circle, where the sun may not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getAlos19Point8Degrees

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getAlos19Point8Degrees

items.title: Alos 26 Degrees
items.text:
This is deprecated as a caution because it is a very early time. It should be used only lechumra, such as stopping eating at this time on a fast day; using it leniently can result in doing mitzvos hayom too early according to most opinions.

Alos, dawn, calculated very early, when the sun is 26° below the eastern geometric horizon before sunrise. It is a degree-based version of the 120-minutes-before-sunrise alos and should be used only lechumra, such as stopping eating from this time on a fast day, not as the start time for mitzvos that can only be performed during the day.

Calculated by the sun’s position: 26° below the eastern geometric horizon before sunrise. This degree is based on the sun’s position 120 minutes before sunrise in Jerusalem around the equinox / equilux, which calculates to 26° below geometric zenith.

This time may not be available in northern or southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle or north of the Antarctic Circle, where the sun may not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getAlos26Degrees

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getAlos26Degrees

items.title: Alos 60 Minutes
items.text:
Alos, dawn, calculated as 60 minutes before sunrise. This is based on the time to walk 4 mil at 15 minutes per mil.

Calculated as 60 fixed minutes before sunrise, or before sea-level sunrise when the elevation setting uses sea level. Time-based offset calculations are based on the view of the Rishonim that the neshef, the time between dawn and sunrise, does not vary by season or location but depends on the time needed to walk 4 mil.

The time depends on the elevation setting: it is counted back from sunrise or from sea-level sunrise.

This time may not be available where sunrise or sunset cannot be calculated, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

The 60-minute calculation is associated in the text with the Chavas Yair in the Mekor Chaim, with additional cited discussions including Chut Hashani Ch. 97, Divrei Malkiel Vol. 4, Ch. 20, page 34, the Maharik Ch. 173, and the Ra'avan.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getAlos60Minutes

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getAlos60Minutes

items.title: Alos 72 Zmanis
items.text:
Alos, dawn, calculated using 72 zmaniyos minutes, meaning 1/10 of the day before sunrise. This calculation is used in calendars published by the Hisachdus Harabanim D'Artzos Habris Ve'Canada.

Calculated as 1.2 GRA shaos zmaniyos before sunrise. This is based on an 18-minute mil, so 4 mil equal 72 minutes, or 1/10 of a 12-hour day. The day is measured from sea-level sunrise to sea-level sunset, or from sunrise to sunset when the elevation setting uses elevation.

The time depends on the elevation setting: the day is based either on sea-level sunrise and sea-level sunset, or on sunrise and sunset using elevation.

This time may not be available where sunrise or sunset cannot be calculated, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getAlos72Zmanis

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getAlos72Zmanis

items.text:

It is based on the time to walk 4 mil at 22.5 minutes per mil, following the view of the Rishonim that the time of Neshef, between dawn and sunrise, does not vary by season or location but depends only on the time needed to walk 4 mil.

90 minutes before sunrise, or before sea-level sunrise depending on the elevation setting.

The time may not be available where the calculation cannot be computed, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Alos, or dawn, calculated as 90 minutes zmaniyos before sunrise. This is ⅛ of the day, based on a 22.5-minute mil, so 4 mil equal 90 minutes.

items.title: Alos Baal Hatanya
items.text:
The Baal Hatanya’s alos, or dawn, based on the sun being 16.9° below the eastern geometric horizon before sunrise. It is based on 72 minutes between dawn and netz amiti, sunrise, the time to walk 4 mil at 18 minutes per mil, associated with Rambam and others.

The time when the sun is 16.9° below the eastern geometric horizon before sunrise. This angle is based on the sun’s position 72 minutes before netz amiti in Jerusalem around the equinox / equilux.

The time may not be available in northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, where the sun may not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

items.title: Alos 16 Point 1 Degrees
items.text:
Alos, or dawn, based on the sun being 16.1° below the eastern geometric horizon before sunrise. It is based on 72 minutes between dawn and sunrise, and between sunset and nightfall, the time to walk 4 mil at 18 minutes per mil, associated with Rambam and others.

The time when the sun is 16.1° below the eastern geometric horizon before sunrise. This angle is based on the sun’s position 72 minutes before sunrise in Jerusalem around the equinox / equilux.

The time may not be available in northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, where the sun may not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

items.title: Alos 72 Minutes
items.text:
Alos, or dawn, calculated as a fixed 72 minutes before sunrise. It is based on the time to walk 4 mil at 18 minutes per mil. The 72-minute time, though not the concept of fixed minutes, is based on the view that Neshef, the twilight between dawn and sunrise, does not vary by season or location but depends on the time needed to walk 4 mil.

72 minutes before sunrise, or before sea-level sunrise depending on the elevation setting.

The time may not be available where the calculation cannot be computed, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

items.title: Bein Hashmashos
items.text: Back to all zmanim

items.title: Bain Hashmashos RT 13 Point 24 Degrees
items.text:
The beginning of bein hashmashos according to Rabbeinu Tam, using a degree-based version of the 58.5-minutes-after-sunset calculation.

It is calculated for when the sun is 13.24° below the western geometric horizon after sunset. This is based on the sun’s position 58.5 minutes after sunset in Jerusalem around the equinox / equilux.

Yisrael Vehazmanim Vol. III page 1028, No. 50 says a dip of slightly less than 13° should be used. The documented calculation uses 13.24°, based on 13.2456° truncated to 13.24°, giving a time about 1.5 seconds earlier.

The difference between 13.24° and 13° is listed as 1 minute 10 seconds in Jerusalem during the equinox, 1 minute 29 seconds in Jerusalem during the solstice, and 1 minute 56 seconds in New York during the solstice.

This time may not be available in northern or southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, where the sun may not go low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getBainHashmashosRT13Point24Degrees

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getBainHashmashosRT13Point24Degrees

items.title: Bain Hashmashos RT 13 Point 5 Minutes Before 7 Point 083 Degrees
items.text:
The beginning of bein hashmashos according to Rabbeinu Tam in this calculation.

It is calculated as 13.5 minutes, which is ¾ of an 18-minute mil, before shkiah calculated as the sun being 7.083° below the horizon.

This time may not be available in northern or southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, where the sun may not go low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getBainHashmashosRT13Point5MinutesBefore7Point083Degrees

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getBainHashmashosRT13Point5MinutesBefore7Point083Degrees

items.title: Bain Hashmashos RT 2 Stars
items.text:
The beginning of bein hashmashos according to Rabbeinu Tam, following the opinion of the Divrei Yosef as cited in Yisrael Vehazmanim.

It is calculated by taking 5/18, or 27.77%, of the time between alos, defined here as when the sun is 19.8° before sunrise, and sunrise, and adding that amount of time to sunset.

This time may not be available in northern or southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, where the sun may not go low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getBainHashmashosRT2Stars

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getBainHashmashosRT2Stars

items.title: Bain Hashmashos RT 58 Point 5 Minutes
items.text:
The beginning of bein hashmashos according to Rabbeinu Tam, calculated as a fixed time after sunset.

It is calculated as 58.5 minutes after sunset. The explanation given is that bein hashmashos is ¾ of a mil before tzais, or 3 ¼ mil after sunset; with a mil of 18 minutes, 3.25 × 18 equals 58.5 minutes.

This time may not be available in places such as the Arctic Circle, where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getBainHashmashosRT58Point5Minutes

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getBainHashmashosRT58Point5Minutes

items.title: Bain Hashmashos Yereim 13 Point 5 Minutes
items.text:
The beginning of bein hashmashos, or twilight, according to the Yereim (Rabbi Eliezer of Metz). According to the Yereim as described here, bein hashmashos starts ¾ of a mil before sunset and tzais, or nightfall, starts at sunset.

It is calculated as 13.5 minutes before sunset, which is ¾ of an 18-minute mil.

This time may not be available in places such as the Arctic Circle, where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getBainHashmashosYereim13Point5Minutes

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getBainHashmashosYereim13Point5Minutes

It is calculated as 16.875 minutes before sunset, which is ¾ of a 22.5-minute mil.

items.text:
The beginning of bein hashmashos according to the Yereim (Rabbi Eliezer of Metz). According to the Yereim as described here, bein hashmashos starts ¾ of a mil before sunset and tzais, or nightfall, starts at sunset.

It is calculated by the sun’s position 2.1° above the horizon around the equinox / equilux in Yerushalayim, corresponding to 13.5 minutes, or ¾ of an 18-minute mil, before sunset.

items.text:
The beginning of bain hashmashos, twilight, according to the Yereim (Rabbi Eliezer of Metz). According to the Yereim, bain hashmashos starts ¾ of a mil before sunset, and tzais, nightfall, starts at sunset.

Calculated when the sun is 2.8° above the horizon around the equinox / equilux, corresponding to 16.875 minutes, or ¾ of an 18-minute mil, before sunset.

This time may not be available when the calculation cannot be computed, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

items.title: Bain Hashmashos Yereim 3 Point 05 Degrees
items.text:
The beginning of bain hashmashos, twilight, according to the Yereim (Rabbi Eliezer of Metz). According to the Yereim, bain hashmashos starts ¾ of a mil before sunset, and tzais, nightfall, starts at sunset.

Calculated when the sun is 3.05° above the horizon around the equinox / equilux, corresponding to 18 minutes, or ¾ of a 24-minute mil, before sunset. A refraction value of 0.5166° is used, described as lechumra by about 14 seconds.

The 0.5166° refraction value is described as more in line with actual refraction in Eretz Yisrael and is cited from Rabbi Yedidya Manet in Zmanei HaHalacha Lema'aseh, p. 11. The same refraction is also used by Luach Itim Lebinah.

For more details, see The Yereim's Bain Hashmashos.

This time may not be available when the calculation cannot be computed, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getBainHashmashosYereim3Point05Degrees

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getBainHashmashosYereim3Point05Degrees

items.text:
The latest time for burning chametz on Erev Pesach according to the Baal Hatanya.

Calculated as 5 Baal Hatanya seasonal hours after netz amiti, sunrise, with the day measured from sunrise to sunset.

This zman is only for Erev Pesach; on other days it may not be available.

It may not be available if the calculation cannot be made, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

items.title: Sof Zman Biur Chametz MGA 16 Point 1 Degrees
items.text:
The latest time for burning chametz on Erev Pesach according to the opinion of the Magen Avraham (MGA), using dawn when the sun is 16.1° below the horizon.

Calculated as 5 seasonal hours after dawn, with the MGA day measured from dawn to nightfall, both defined as the sun being 16.1° below sunrise or sunset.

This zman is only for Erev Pesach; on other days it may not be available.

It may not be available if the calculation cannot be made, such as in northern and southern locations even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle where the sun may not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

items.title: Sof Zman Shma 3 Hours Before Chatzos
items.text:
The latest time to recite Shema in the morning, calculated as 3 regular clock hours before chatzos hayom. This is generally known as part of the “Komarno” zmanim after Rav Yitzchak Eizik of Komarno, a proponent of this calculation, though the documentation notes that it predates him.

3 regular clock hours, not shaos zmaniyos, before chatzos hayom.

This zman may not be available when the calculation cannot be computed, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getSofZmanShma3HoursBeforeChatzos

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getSofZmanShma3HoursBeforeChatzos

items.title: Sof Zman Shma Alos 16 Point 1 To Sunset
items.text:
The latest time to recite Shema in the morning based on the opinion that the day starts at alos 16.1° and ends at sea level sunset. This is the opinion of the חידושי וכללות הרז״ה and the מנורה הטהורה as mentioned by Yisrael Vehazmanim vol. 1, sec. 7, ch. 3 no. 16.

3 shaos zmaniyos are calculated from a day that runs from alos 16.1° to sea level sunset, and added to alos.

Based on this calculation, chatzos will not be at midday.

This zman may not be available in northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, where the sun may not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getSofZmanShmaAlos16Point1ToSunset

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getSofZmanShmaAlos16Point1ToSunset

items.text:

3 shaos zmaniyos are calculated from a day that runs from alos 16.1° to tzais 7.083°, and added to alos 16.1°.

Based on this calculation, chatzos will not be at midday.

This zman may not be available in northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, where the sun may not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

items.title: Sof Zman Shma Ateret Torah
items.text:
The latest time to recite Shema in the morning based on the calculation of Chacham Yosef Harari-Raful of Yeshivat Ateret Torah.

The day starts 1/10th of the day before sunrise and is usually calculated as ending 40 minutes after sunset. Shaos zmaniyos are calculated based on this day, and 3 shaos zmaniyos are added to alos.

The end of the day is configurable to an offset other than 40 minutes after sunset.

Based on this calculation, chatzos will not be at midday.

This zman may not be available when the calculation cannot be computed, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

items.text:
Rav Moshe Feinstein’s opinion for the latest time to recite Shema in the morning according to the GRA, where the day is calculated from sunrise to sunset, but using only the first half of the day.

The half-day starts at sunrise and ends at fixed local chatzos. Sof zman Shema is 3 shaos zmaniyos after sunrise, which is half of this half-day.

This zman may not be available in northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, where the sun may not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

items.title: Sof Zman Tfila MGA 18 Degrees
items.text:
The latest time to recite the morning prayers according to the Magen Avraham (MGA), based on alos when the sun is 18° below the horizon before sunrise.

The day is calculated from dawn to nightfall, with both defined as when the sun is 18° below the horizon before sunrise or after sunset. The time is 4 shaos zmaniyos after dawn.

This time may not be available if the calculation cannot be computed, such as in northern and southern locations even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle where the sun may not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getSofZmanTfilaMGA18Degrees

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getSofZmanTfilaMGA18Degrees

items.title: Sof Zman Tfila MGA 72 Minutes Zmanis
items.text:
The latest time for the morning prayers according to the Magen Avraham (MGA), using a dawn that is 72 proportional minutes before sunrise.

It is 4 proportional solar hours after dawn. The day is measured from dawn, 72 proportional minutes before sunrise, until nightfall, 72 proportional minutes after sunset.

This time may not be available when the calculation cannot be made, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getSofZmanTfilaMGA72MinutesZmanis

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getSofZmanTfilaMGA72MinutesZmanis

items.title: Sof Zman Tfila GRA
items.text:
The latest time for morning prayer according to the GRA.

It is 4 proportional solar hours after sunrise. The day is measured from sunrise to sunset, using either regular sunrise and sunset or sea-level sunrise and sunset depending on the elevation setting.

This time depends on whether the elevation setting is used.

This time may not be available when the calculation cannot be made, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

items.text:

It is 4 proportional solar hours after dawn. The day is measured from 72 minutes before sunrise to 72 minutes after sunset, with sunrise and sunset based on the elevation setting.

This time depends on whether the elevation setting is used.

This time may not be available when the calculation cannot be made, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

items.title: Kidush Levana
items.text: Back to all zmanim

items.title: Sof Zman Kidush Levana 15 Days
items.text:
The latest time for Kiddush Levana, calculated as 15 days after the molad. This follows the opinion of the Shulchan Aruch, Orach Chaim 426.

It is the moment 15 days after the molad.

Some opinions understand the Rema, citing the Maharil, as using the halfway-between-molads calculation, and as holding that the Mechaber agrees. The Aruch Hashulchan is also noted. For more detail, see Rabbi Dovid Heber’s write-up in Siman Daled, chapter 4, of Shaarei Zmanim.

This time is given even if it occurs during the day.

If the time falls between alos and tzais, alos is used.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getSofZmanKidushLevana15Days

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getSofZmanKidushLevana15Days

items.title: Sof Zman Kidush Levana Between Moldos
items.text:
The latest time for Kiddush Levana according to the Maharil, who calculates it as halfway from one molad to the next.

It adds half of the 29 days, 12 hours, and 793 chalakim between one molad and the next to the month’s molad. This half-period is 14 days, 18 hours, 22 minutes, and 666 milliseconds.

This time is given even if it occurs during the day.

If the time falls between alos and tzais, alos is used.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getSofZmanKidushLevanaBetweenMoldos

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getSofZmanKidushLevanaBetweenMoldos

items.title: Tchilas Zman Kidush Levana 3 Days
items.text:
The earliest time for Kiddush Levana according to Rabbeinu Yonah, who holds that it can be said 3 days after the molad.

It is the moment 3 days after the molad.

This time is given even if it occurs during the day, when Kiddush Levana cannot be said.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTchilasZmanKidushLevana3Days

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTchilasZmanKidushLevana3Days

items.title: Tchilas Zman Kidush Levana 7 Days
items.text:
The earliest time for Kiddush Levana according to opinions that it should not be said until 7 days after the molad.

It is the moment 7 days after the molad, whether it is day or night.

This time is given even if it occurs during the day, when Kiddush Levana cannot be recited.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTchilasZmanKidushLevana7Days

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTchilasZmanKidushLevana7Days

items.title: Chatzos Hayom
items.text: Back to all zmanim

items.title: Sun Transit
items.text:
Solar noon, also called sundial noon, is when the Sun is transiting the celestial meridian. See The Definition of Chatzos for more detail on the definition of solar noon or midday.

Using the NOAA calculation, this is calculated as astronomical noon.

Technical details

Source method: AstronomicalCalendar.getSunTransit

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.AstronomicalCalendar.getSunTransit

items.title: Fixed Local Chatzos Hayom
items.text:
Fixed local chatzos is a local noon time adjusted from standard time based on the local longitude. This is the time of chatzos according to the Aruch Hashulchan in Orach Chaim 233:14 and Rabbi Moshe Feinstein in Igros Moshe Orach Chaim 1:24 and 2:20.

The globe’s 360° divided by 24 hours gives 15° per hour, or 4 minutes per degree. At longitudes 0°, 15°, 30°, and so on, chatzos is exactly 12:00 noon. Other longitudes are adjusted by their distance from the nearest 15° multiple, at 4 minutes per degree. For example, Lakewood, N.J., at longitude -74.222, is 0.778° from -75°; multiplied by 4, this gives 3 minutes and 7 seconds, for a chatzos of 11:56:53.

This time is not tied to theoretical 15° time zones; it adjusts to the actual time zone and Daylight saving time.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getFixedLocalChatzosHayom

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getFixedLocalChatzosHayom

items.title: Chatzos Hayom
items.text:
Chatzos hayom is the daytime midpoint used for chatzos. With the NOAA calculation, it can be astronomical chatzos, described as a more accurate solar-noon calculation. See The Definition of Chatzos for a detailed explanation of ways to calculate chatzos.

With the NOAA calculation, chatzos hayom is calculated as astronomical chatzos when the setting to use astronomical chatzos is enabled. If that setting is not enabled, it is calculated as the halfway point between sunrise and sunset.

If the halfway-between-sunrise-and-sunset calculation cannot be made, such as in the Arctic Circle on a day when sunrise or sunset did not occur, astronomical chatzos may still be used when supported in order to avoid an unavailable time.

Technical details

Source method: ZmanimCalendar.getChatzosHayom

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ZmanimCalendar.getChatzosHayom

items.title: Chatzos Hayom As Half Day
items.text:
This is chatzos calculated as halfway between sunrise and sunset. Many are of the opinion that chatzos is the midpoint between sea-level sunrise and sea-level sunset, despite this not being the most accurate way to calculate it. See The Definition of Chatzos for a detailed explanation of ways to calculate chatzos.

It is calculated as the halfway point between sea-level sunrise and sea-level sunset, which is the same as six shaos zmaniyos after sunrise. A day that starts at alos and ends at tzais using the same time or degree offset will also produce the same time.

Because days lengthen or shorten, this is not necessarily the exact midpoint of the day in reality, but it is very close.

This time may not be available when it cannot be calculated, such as in the Arctic Circle on a day when the sun does not rise or does not set.

Technical details

Source method: ZmanimCalendar.getChatzosHayomAsHalfDay

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ZmanimCalendar.getChatzosHayomAsHalfDay

items.title: Midnight
items.text: Back to all zmanim

items.title: Solar Midnight
items.text:
Solar midnight is the Sun’s lower transit at the end of the day being calculated. It may occur after clock midnight; for example, for February 8 it refers to the midnight between February 8 and February 9. It is when the Sun is transiting the lower celestial meridian, or when the Sun is at its nadir.

Using the NOAA calculator, this is calculated as astronomical midnight. See The Definition of Chatzos for details on the definition of solar noon and midday.

With the NOAA calculator, this time is never expected to be unavailable.

Technical details

Source method: AstronomicalCalendar.getSolarMidnight

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.AstronomicalCalendar.getSolarMidnight

items.title: Chatzos Halayla
items.text:
Chatzos halayla is the midnight point at the end of the day being calculated, and it may actually be after clock midnight. For example, when calculating it for Erev Pesach, it is calculated for Lail Pesach so it can be used as the zman for sof zman achilas chametz.

Using the NOAA calculator, it is calculated more accurately as astronomical chatzos halayla when astronomical chatzos is enabled. If astronomical chatzos is not enabled, it is calculated as the halfway point between sunset and the following day’s sunrise. See The Definition of Chatzos for a detailed explanation of ways to calculate chatzos.

This time depends on whether astronomical chatzos is enabled.

The halfway-between-sunset-and-next-sunrise calculation may not be available in the Arctic on a day when either sunset or the following day’s sunrise did not happen. With the NOAA calculator, astronomical chatzos halayla can still be calculated in that situation.

Technical details

Source method: ZmanimCalendar.getChatzosHalayla

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ZmanimCalendar.getChatzosHalayla

items.title: Mincha Gedola
items.text: Back to all zmanim

items.title: Mincha Gedola 16 Point 1 Degrees
items.text:
Mincha gedola according to the Magen Avraham, with the day beginning and ending when the sun is 16.1° below the horizon. It is described as the earliest time to pray mincha.

Calculated as 6.5 solar hours after alos, using a day that begins at alos 16.1°. If the setting to use astronomical chatzos for other zmanim is enabled, it is calculated as half of a half-day shaah zmanis between chatzos hayom and alos 16.1°, after chatzos hayom.

This time may not be available in northern or southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle or north of the Antarctic Circle, where the sun may not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMinchaGedola16Point1Degrees

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMinchaGedola16Point1Degrees

items.title: Mincha Gedola 30 Minutes
items.text:
Mincha gedola calculated as 30 regular clock minutes after chatzos. Some use this time to delay the start of mincha in the winter when half of a shaah zmanis is less than 30 minutes.

Calculated as a fixed 30 minutes after chatzos, rather than half of a shaah zmanis after chatzos.

One should not use this time to start mincha before the standard GRA mincha gedola.

See Shulchan Aruch Orach Chayim 234:1 and the Shaar Hatziyon seif katan ches.

Because this is a fixed 30 minutes after chatzos, it is affected by the setting for astronomical chatzos, not by the setting for using astronomical chatzos for other zmanim.

This time may not be available in places such as the Arctic Circle, where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMinchaGedola30Minutes

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMinchaGedola30Minutes

items.title: Mincha Gedola 72 Minutes
items.text:
Mincha gedola according to the Magen Avraham, with the day beginning 72 minutes before sunrise and ending 72 minutes after sunset. It is described as the earliest time to pray mincha.

Calculated as 6.5 solar hours after alos, using a day that begins 72 minutes before sunrise. If the setting to use astronomical chatzos for other zmanim is enabled, it is calculated as half of a half-day shaah zmanis between chatzos hayom and tzais 72 minutes, after chatzos hayom.

This time may not be available in places such as the Arctic Circle, where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMinchaGedola72Minutes

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMinchaGedola72Minutes

items.title: Mincha Gedola Ahavat Shalom
items.text:
Mincha gedola based on the opinion of Rabbi Yaakov Moshe Hillel, as published in the luach of the Bais Horaah of Yeshivat Chevrat Ahavat Shalom. Mincha gedola is described as the earliest time to pray mincha.

Calculated as half a shaah zmanis after chatzos, with shaos zmaniyos based on a day starting at alos 16.1° and ending at tzais 3.7°, 13.5 minutes after sunset. The time used is the later of this calculation or 30 regular clock minutes after chatzos.

The setting to use astronomical chatzos for other zmanim does not apply here, because this zman involves chatzos offset from the center of the astronomical day.

This time may not be available in northern or southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle or north of the Antarctic Circle, where the sun may not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMinchaGedolaAhavatShalom

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMinchaGedolaAhavatShalom

items.title: Mincha Gedola Ateret Torah
items.text:
Mincha gedola based on the calculation of Chacham Yosef Harari-Raful of Yeshivat Ateret Torah. It is described as the preferred earliest time to pray mincha according to the opinion of the Rambam and others.

Calculated as 6.5 solar hours after alos, where the day starts 1/10th of the day before sunrise and usually ends 40 minutes after sunset.

The end of the day is usually 40 minutes after sunset, but that offset is configurable to any offset.

This time may not be available in places such as the Arctic Circle, where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMinchaGedolaAteretTorah

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMinchaGedolaAteretTorah

items.title: Mincha Gedola Baal Hatanya
items.text:
Mincha gedola according to the Baal Hatanya. Mincha gedola is described as the earliest time one can pray mincha. The Rambam is of the opinion that it is better to delay mincha until mincha ketana, while the Ra"sh, Tur, GRA, and others are of the opinion that mincha can be prayed lechatchila starting at mincha gedola.

Calculated as 6.5 sea-level solar hours after netz amiti, sunrise, based on the Baal Hatanya’s opinion that the day is calculated from sunrise to sunset.

This time may not be available in places such as the Arctic Circle, where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMinchaGedolaBaalHatanya

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMinchaGedolaBaalHatanya

items.title: Mincha Gedola GRAFixed Local Chatzos 30 Minutes
items.text:
Rav Moshe Feinstein’s calculation of mincha gedola, the earliest time one can pray mincha according to the GRA.

Calculated as 30 minutes after fixed local chatzos.

This time may not be available in places such as the Arctic Circle, where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMinchaGedolaGRAFixedLocalChatzos30Minutes

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMinchaGedolaGRAFixedLocalChatzos30Minutes

items.title: Mincha Gedola GRAGreater Than 30
items.text:
The later of GRA mincha gedola and mincha gedola calculated as 30 minutes after chatzos. In the winter, when half of a GRA shaah zmanis is less than 30 minutes, the 30-minutes-after-chatzos time is used; otherwise, the GRA mincha gedola time is used.

Calculated by comparing GRA mincha gedola with 30 minutes after chatzos and using the later time.

Because this calculation can be an offset from chatzos when 30 clock minutes is later than half of a shaah zmanis, it may be affected by the setting for astronomical chatzos.

This time may not be available in places such as the Arctic Circle, where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMinchaGedolaGRAGreaterThan30

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMinchaGedolaGRAGreaterThan30

items.title: Mincha Gedola GRA
items.text:
The latest mincha gedola according to the GRA. Mincha gedola is the earliest time one can pray mincha. The Ramba"m holds that it is better to delay mincha until mincha ketana GRA, while the Ra"sh, Tur, GRA, and others hold that mincha can be prayed lechatchila starting at mincha gedola.

Calculated as 6.5 shaos zmaniyos, or solar hours, after sunrise. The day is measured from sunrise to sunset, using either sea-level sunrise and sea-level sunset or elevation-adjusted sunrise and sunset, depending on the elevation setting.

This time may not be available when the calculation cannot be computed, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: ZmanimCalendar.getMinchaGedolaGRA

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ZmanimCalendar.getMinchaGedolaGRA

items.title: Mincha Ketana
items.text: Back to all zmanim

items.title: Mincha Ketana 16 Point 1 Degrees
items.text:
Mincha ketana according to the Magen Avraham, with the day starting and ending when the sun is 16.1° below the horizon. This is described as the preferred earliest time to pray mincha according to the Rambam and others.

Calculated as 9.5 solar hours after alos, using alos at 16.1° below the horizon and solar hours based on that same 16.1° day.

This time may not be available in northern or southern locations where the sun does not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMinchaKetana16Point1Degrees

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMinchaKetana16Point1Degrees

items.title: Mincha Ketana 72 Minutes
items.text:
Mincha ketana according to the Magen Avraham, with the day starting 72 minutes before sunrise and ending 72 minutes after sunset. This is described as the preferred earliest time to pray mincha according to the Rambam and others.

Calculated as 9.5 solar hours after alos, using alos 72 minutes before sunrise and solar hours based on a day from 72 minutes before sunrise to 72 minutes after sunset.

This time may not be available in places such as the Arctic Circle, where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMinchaKetana72Minutes

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMinchaKetana72Minutes

items.title: Mincha Ketana Ahavat Shalom
items.text:
Mincha ketana based on the opinion of Rabbi Yaakov Moshe Hillel, as published in the luach of the Bais Horaah of Yeshivat Chevrat Ahavat Shalom. Mincha ketana is described as the preferred earliest time to pray mincha according to the Rambam and others.

Calculated as 2.5 shaos zmaniyos before tzais at 3.8°, with shaos zmaniyos based on a day from alos at 16.1° to tzais at 3.8°.

This time may not be available in northern or southern locations where the sun does not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMinchaKetanaAhavatShalom

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMinchaKetanaAhavatShalom

items.title: Mincha Ketana Ateret Torah
items.text:
Mincha ketana based on the calculation of Chacham Yosef Harari-Raful of Yeshivat Ateret Torah. This is described as the preferred earliest time to pray mincha according to the Rambam and others.

Calculated as 9.5 solar hours after alos. In this calculation, the day starts 1/10th of the day before sunrise and usually ends 40 minutes after sunset.

The end of the day is usually 40 minutes after sunset, but the offset can be configured to another value.

This time may not be available in places such as the Arctic Circle, where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMinchaKetanaAteretTorah

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMinchaKetanaAteretTorah

items.title: Mincha Ketana Baal Hatanya
items.text:
Mincha ketana according to the Baal Hatanya. This is described as the preferred earliest time to pray mincha according to the Rambam and others.

Calculated as 9.5 sea-level solar hours after netz amiti, sunrise. This follows the Baal Hatanya’s view that the day is calculated from sunrise to sunset.

This time may not be available in places such as the Arctic Circle, where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMinchaKetanaBaalHatanya

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMinchaKetanaBaalHatanya

items.title: Mincha Ketana GRAFixed Local Chatzos To Sunset
items.text:
Mincha ketana according to Rav Moshe Feinstein, calculated according to the GRA. It is described as the preferred time to recite the mincha prayers according to the Rambam and others.

Calculated as 3.5 shaos zmaniyos, solar hours, after fixed local chatzos.

This time may not be available in places such as the Arctic Circle, where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMinchaKetanaGRAFixedLocalChatzosToSunset

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMinchaKetanaGRAFixedLocalChatzosToSunset

items.title: Samuch Le Mincha Ketana 16 Point 1 Degrees
items.text:
Samuch lemincha ketana means near mincha ketana. It is described as the time before praying mincha when eating or other activity cannot begin. See the Mechaber and Mishna Berurah 232 and 249:2.

Calculated as 9 solar hours after alos at 16.1° below the horizon, based on a day from alos at 16.1° to tzais at 16.1°. It is also described as half an hour before mincha ketana.

This time may not be available in northern or southern locations where the sun does not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getSamuchLeMinchaKetana16Point1Degrees

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getSamuchLeMinchaKetana16Point1Degrees

items.title: Samuch Le Mincha Ketana 72 Minutes
items.text:
Samuch lemincha ketana means near mincha ketana. It is described as the time before praying mincha when eating or other activity cannot begin. See the Mechaber and Mishna Berurah 232 and 249:2.

Calculated as 9 solar hours after alos 72 minutes before sunrise, based on a day from alos 72 minutes before sunrise to tzais 72 minutes after sunset. It is also described as half an hour before mincha ketana.

This time may not be available in northern or southern locations where the sun does not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getSamuchLeMinchaKetana72Minutes

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getSamuchLeMinchaKetana72Minutes

items.title: Samuch Le Mincha Ketana GRA
items.text:
Samuch lemincha ketana means “near mincha ketana,” the time half an hour before mincha ketana. It is described as the time before which eating or other activity cannot begin prior to praying mincha. This calculation follows the GRA.

It is calculated as 9 shaos zmaniyos, or solar hours, after sunrise, using a day that starts at sunrise and ends at sunset. Sunrise may be elevation-adjusted depending on the elevation setting.

The time may not be available when the calculation cannot be computed, such as in some northern or southern locations where the sun may not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

See the Mechaber and Mishna Berurah 232 and 249:2.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getSamuchLeMinchaKetanaGRA

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getSamuchLeMinchaKetanaGRA

items.title: Mincha Ketana GRA
items.text:
Mincha ketana is the preferred earliest time to pray mincha according to the Rambam and others. This calculation follows the GRA.

It is calculated as 9.5 shaos zmaniyos, or solar hours, after sunrise. The day is measured from sunrise to sunset, or from sea-level sunrise to sea-level sunset, depending on the elevation setting.

The time may not be available when the calculation cannot be computed, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: ZmanimCalendar.getMinchaKetanaGRA

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ZmanimCalendar.getMinchaKetanaGRA

items.title: Misheyakir 12 Point 85 Degrees
items.text:
Deprecated because this is a very early misheyakir time that should only be used bish’as hadchak. Lechatchila, a later zman should be used.

Misheyakir based on a very early time mentioned for use bish’as hadchak in Birur Halacha Tinyana and Tlisa’ah in Orach Chaim siman 18 as 12.85°. It is based on the sun’s position slightly later than 57 minutes before sunrise in Jerusalem around the equinox / equilux.

Calculated when the sun is 12.85° below geometric zenith, meaning 12.85° below the horizon.

Actual calculations show 57 minutes before sunrise in Jerusalem to be slightly more than 12.9°, while Birur Halacha indicates 12.85° as a slight chumra on a bedieved time, a difference of about 14 seconds at the equinox/equilux in Jerusalem.

A 12.9° time is also mentioned in Piskei Tshuvos siman 18, page 190, where a typo identifies it as the degree equivalent of 60 minutes before sunrise, though at that point the sun is about 13.5° below the horizon.

The 57-minute time is mentioned by the Minchas Yitzchak vol. 9, siman 9 as 15 minutes before alos hashachar, though no location or degree-based conversion is specified there.

The Kaf Hachaim vol. 1 siman 18, no. 18 states that in Yerushalayim 60 fixed minutes are used year-round. Calculations show that 60 fixed minutes in Yerushalayim ranges from 13.5° at the spring equinox to 11.5° at the summer solstice, and 57-minute misheyakir ranges from 12.9° at the winter equinox to 11° at the summer solstice.

The difference between 12.85° and 12.9° is usually small in the examples given: up to 23 seconds in Lakewood, NJ; about 16 seconds there in winter; up to 29 seconds in Montreal; and about 18 seconds there in winter.

At the latitude of Vilnius, around the summer solstice the sun may not reach this far below the horizon. On dates when both 12.85° and 12.9° can be calculated, the difference can exceed two minutes for about a week, while from December through March it is about 22 seconds.

In some northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, the sun may not go low enough below the horizon for this calculation, so the time may not be available.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMisheyakir12Point85Degrees

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMisheyakir12Point85Degrees

items.title: Misheyakir 7 Point 65 Degrees
items.text:
Misheyakir based on a 35/36-minute time around the equinox / equilux, when neshef, or twilight, is shortest. It is based on Rabbi Moshe Feinstein, who writes in Ohr Hachaim Vol. 4, Ch. 6 that misheyakir in New York is 35–40 minutes before sunrise, a drop less than 8°.

Calculated when the sun is 7.65° below geometric zenith, meaning 7.65° below the horizon.

Rabbi Yisroel Taplin, in Zmanei Yisrael, notes that Rabbi Yaakov Kamenetsky stated that it is not less than 36 minutes before sunrise, and maybe 40 minutes.

Sefer Yisrael Vehazmanim quotes the Tamar Yifrach in the name of the Satmar Rov that one should be stringent not to consider misheyakir before 36 minutes.

This is also described as the accepted minhag in Lakewood used in the Yeshiva, following Rabbi Shmuel Kamenetsky, who gave the time as 35/36 minutes but did not provide a degree-based time.

Because this zman depends on the level of light, Rabbi Yaakov Shakow presented this degree-based calculation to Rabbi Shmuel Kamenetsky, who agreed to it.

In some northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, the sun may not go low enough below the horizon for this calculation, so the time may not be available.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMisheyakir7Point65Degrees

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMisheyakir7Point65Degrees

items.title: Misheyakir 9 Point 5 Degrees
items.text:
Misheyakir based on Rabbi Dovid Kronglass’s calculation of 45 minutes in Baltimore, as mentioned in Divrei Chachamim No. 24 and brought by Birur Halacha, Tinyana, Ch. 18. This calculates to 9.5°.

Calculated when the sun is 9.5° below geometric zenith, meaning 9.5° below the horizon.

Rabbi Yaakov Yitzchok Neiman, in Kovetz Eitz Chaim Vol. 9, p. 202, is cited regarding the Vya’an Yosef not wanting to rely on times earlier than 45 minutes in New York.

This zman is also used in calendars published by Rabbi Hershel Edelstein. Yisroel Vehazmanim states that Rabbi Edelstein received the 45-minute zman from Rabbi Bick.

Calendars published by the Edot Hamizrach communities also use this zman.

This also follows Rabbi Shmuel Kamenetsky, who provided times of 36 and 45 minutes but did not provide a degree-based time. Because this zman depends on the level of light, Rabbi Yaakov Shakow presented these degree-based times to Rabbi Shmuel Kamenetsky, who agreed to them.

In some northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, the sun may not go low enough below the horizon for this calculation, so the time may not be available.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMisheyakir9Point5Degrees

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMisheyakir9Point5Degrees

items.title: Plag Ahavat Shalom
items.text:
Plag hamincha, described here as the earliest time that Shabbos can be started, according to Rabbi Yaakov Moshe Hillel as published in the luach of the Bais Horaah of Yeshivat Chevrat Ahavat Shalom.

It is calculated as 1.25 shaos zmaniyos before tzais at 3.8°, with the shaos zmaniyos based on a day that starts at alos 16.1° and ends at tzais 3.8°.

This time may not be available in northern or southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, where the sun may not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

It is calculated as 10.75 shaos zmaniyos after alos 16.1°, with the shaos zmaniyos based on the day from alos 16.1° to tzais 7.083°.

This time may not be available in northern or southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, where the sun may not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

items.title: Plag Alos To Sunset
items.text:
Deprecated because it should be used lechumra only. It gives a very late time, often after shkiah, and using it lekula can result in chillul Shabbos and similar concerns.

Plag hamincha based on the opinion that the day starts at alos 16.1° and ends at sea level sunset.

It is calculated as 10.75 shaos zmaniyos after alos 16.1°, with the shaos zmaniyos based on the day from alos 16.1° before sunrise to sea level sunset.

This calculation can place plag after sunset, so it should be used lechumra only.

This time may not be available in northern or southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, where the sun may not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

items.title: Plag Hamincha 120 Minutes
items.text:
Deprecated because it should be used lechumra only. It gives a very late time, often after shkiah, and using it lekula can result in chillul Shabbos and similar concerns.

Plag hamincha according to the Magen Avraham, with the day starting at alos 120 minutes. It should be used lechumra only.

It is calculated as 10.75 shaos zmaniyos after alos 120 minutes.

Because this zman is based on an extremely early alos and a very late tzais, it gives a very late time, often after shkiah, and can result in chillul Shabbos and similar concerns if not treated as lechumra.

This time may not be available in the Arctic Circle, where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

items.title: Plag Hamincha Ateret Torah
items.text:
Plag hamincha according to Chacham Yosef Harari-Raful of Yeshivat Ateret Torah.

The day starts at alos, defined as 1/10 of the day before sunrise, and usually ends 40 minutes after sunset. Shaos zmaniyos are based on that day, and plag hamincha is 10.75 shaos zmaniyos after alos.

The end of the day is configurable to an offset other than 40 minutes after sunset.

This time may not be available when the calculation cannot be computed, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getPlagHaminchaAteretTorah

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getPlagHaminchaAteretTorah

items.title: Plag Hamincha Baal Hatanya
items.text:
Plag hamincha according to the Baal Hatanya, where the day is calculated from sunrise to sunset.

It is calculated as 10.75 shaos zmaniyos after netz amiti, sunrise. More details are available at About Our Zmanim Calculations @ Chabad.org.

This time may not be available when the calculation cannot be computed, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getPlagHaminchaBaalHatanya

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getPlagHaminchaBaalHatanya

items.text:
Plag hamincha according to Rav Moshe Feinstein’s opinion, calculated according to the GRA.

The day ends at sunset, and plag hamincha is 4.75 shaos zmaniyos after fixed local chatzos.

This time may not be available when the calculation cannot be computed, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

items.title: Plag Hamincha GRA
items.text:
Plag hamincha according to the GRA. Plag hamincha is the earliest time that Shabbos can be started.

It is calculated as 10.75 shaos zmaniyos after sunrise. The day is calculated from sunrise to sunset, with the exact sunrise and sunset depending on whether elevation is used.

This time may not be available when the calculation cannot be computed, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: ZmanimCalendar.getPlagHaminchaGRA

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ZmanimCalendar.getPlagHaminchaGRA

items.title: Sea Level Sunrise
items.text:
Sunrise calculated as if the location were at sea level, without any elevation adjustment.

This is the sea-level sunrise time. It is used as the basis for dawn calculations that are based on how far the sun is below the horizon before sunrise.

Dawn and dusk depend on the amount of visible light, which is not affected by elevation.

This time may not be available when the calculation cannot be made, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: AstronomicalCalendar.getSeaLevelSunrise

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.AstronomicalCalendar.getSeaLevelSunrise

items.title: Sunrise
items.text:
Sunrise adjusted for the location’s elevation.

The calculation uses a geometric zenith of 90° plus the elevation adjustment, and also accounts for approximately 34 arcminutes of refraction and 16 arcminutes for the sun’s radius, for a total adjustment to 90.83333°.

This time may not be available when the calculation cannot be made, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: AstronomicalCalendar.getSunrise

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.AstronomicalCalendar.getSunrise

items.title: Sunrise Or Easternmost Solar Azimuth
items.text:
Sunrise if it occurs that day; otherwise, in polar regions, the time when the sun is at its easternmost position, azimuth 90°.

If there is no sunrise that day, this uses the time when the sun reaches azimuth 90°. This time is close to six hours before astronomical chatzos hayom, but depending on the time of year and location in the Arctic or Antarctic, it can be up to 46 minutes before or after that time.

This applies to polar regions, the Arctic or Antarctic circles, where there can be days with no sunrise or sunset.

The related sunset opinion uses the sun’s westernmost position, azimuth 270°.

This opinion is attributed to Rabbi Yehosef Schwarz in דברי יוסף – דרך מבוא השמש and דברי יוסף – תשובות, שאלה ח׳, and is brought down lehalacha by the Ben Ish Chai in רב פעלים – חלק ב׳, סוד ישרים ס׳ ד׳.

If there is no sunrise that day and the sun does not reach azimuth 90°, this time may not be available.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getSunriseOrEasternmostSolarAzimuth

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getSunriseOrEasternmostSolarAzimuth

items.title: Sea Level Sunset
items.text:
Sunset calculated as if the location were at sea level, without any elevation adjustment.

This is the sea-level sunset time. It is used as the base for dusk calculations that are measured as the sun’s dip below the horizon after sunset.

This time may not be available when the calculation cannot be computed, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: AstronomicalCalendar.getSeaLevelSunset

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.AstronomicalCalendar.getSeaLevelSunset

items.title: Sunset
items.text:
Sunset adjusted for the location’s elevation.

The calculation uses a geometric zenith of 90° plus the elevation adjustment. It also accounts for about 34 arcminutes of refraction and 16 arcminutes for the sun’s radius, for a total of 90.83333°.

In certain cases, sunset can be calculated before sunrise, typically when a time zone other than the local time zone is used. In that case, the sunset date is moved to the following date.

This time may not be available when the calculation cannot be computed, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

Technical details

Source method: AstronomicalCalendar.getSunset

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.AstronomicalCalendar.getSunset

items.title: Sunset Or Westernmost Solar Azimuth
items.text:
Sunset when it occurs, or otherwise the time when the sun reaches its westernmost position, at azimuth 270°.

In polar regions, there are days with no sunrise or sunset. Some opinions use the sun’s westernmost position as the day-night boundary during these periods. This time is close to six hours after astronomical chatzos hayom, but depending on the time of year and location in the Arctic or Antarctic, it can be up to 46 minutes before or after that time.

The opinion described is attributed to Rabbi Yehosef Schwarz in דברי יוסף – דרך מבוא השמש and דברי יוסף – תשובות, שאלה ח׳, and is brought down lehalacha by the Ben Ish Chai in רב פעלים – חלק ב׳, סוד ישרים ס׳ ד׳.

If there is no sunset that day and azimuth 270° also does not occur, this time may not be available.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getSunsetOrWesternmostSolarAzimuth

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getSunsetOrWesternmostSolarAzimuth

items.title: Tzais 120 Minutes
items.text:
Deprecated because it gives a very late time and should be used only lechumra; using it lekula can result in chillul Shabbos and similar problems.

Tzais, or nightfall, according to Rav Chaim Naeh, based on the view that a mil according to the Rambam is 24 minutes, and on Ula’s calculation of tzais as 5 mil after sunset.

Calculated as 120 minutes after sunset, or after sea-level sunset depending on the elevation setting.

This time is extremely late and is long past the 18° point where the darkest point is reached. It should be used only lechumra, such as for delaying the start of nighttime mitzvos.

The time may not be available when the calculation cannot be computed, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

items.title: Tzais 120 Zmanis
items.text:
Deprecated because it gives a very late time and should be used only lechumra; using it lekula can result in chillul Shabbos and similar problems.

Tzais, or dusk, calculated using 120 zmaniyos minutes after sea-level sunset.

Calculated as 120 zmaniyos minutes after sea-level sunset.

This time is extremely late and, in most places on the globe, is when the sun is well below the 18° point described as the scientifically darkest point. It should be used only lechumra, such as for delaying the start of nighttime mitzvos.

The time may not be available when the calculation cannot be computed, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

items.title: Tzais 16 Point 1 Degrees
items.text:
Tzais at the point when the sun is 16.1° below the horizon. This corresponds to the sun’s position 72 minutes after sunset according to Rabbeinu Tam’s calculation of tzais around the equinox / equilux in Jerusalem.

Calculated when the sun is 16.1° below the horizon.

The question of equinox versus equilux is described as complex. Rabbi Meir Posen in Ohr Meir is cited as favoring the equilux, while Rabbi Yedidya Manet in Zmanei HaHalacha Lema'aseh and Rabbi Yonah Mertzbuch are cited as favoring the astronomical equinox. The difference is about 9 seconds.

The time may not be available in northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, where the sun may not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTzais16Point1Degrees

items.title: Tzais 72 Zmanis
items.text:
Tzais, or dusk, according to a calculation of Rabbeinu Tam’s time of tzeis described by the Minchas Cohen in Ma’amar 2:4.

Calculated as 72 zmaniyos minutes, equal to 1/10 of the day, after sea level sunset.

This calculation makes the shortest time from sunset to tzais occur at the winter solstice, the longest at the summer solstice, and 72 clock minutes at the equinox.

The source notes that this does not match the actual pattern of twilight, since there is no direct relationship between the length of the day and twilight.

This time may not be available where the calculation cannot be computed, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

items.title: Tzais Ateret Torah
items.text:
Tzais associated with Chacham Yosef Harari-Raful of Yeshivat Ateret Torah, who uses this time only for calculating other zmanei hayom such as Sof Zman Krias Shema and Plag Hamincha. His calendars do not publish a zman for Tzais.

Usually calculated as 40 minutes after sea level sunset. The offset is configurable to any number of minutes.

Chacham Harari-Raful provided a 25-minute zman for Israel, while the default described here is 40 minutes year-round in any place on the globe.

This time may not be available where the calculation cannot be computed, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

items.title: Tzais Baal Hatanya
items.text:
Tzais, or nightfall, based on the opinion of the Baal Hatanya.

Calculated when the sun is 6° below the western geometric horizon after sunset. This is based on the sun’s position about 24 minutes after sea level sunset in Jerusalem around the equinox / equilux. The source also references About Our Zmanim Calculations @ Chabad.org, where shkiah amitis is treated as 1.583° below the horizon, with added time totaling a calculation close to 5.83° below the horizon and rounded up to 6°.

This time may not be available in northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, where the sun may not reach low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

items.title: Tzais 72 Minutes
items.text:
Tzais hakochavim, or nightfall, based on the opinion of Rabbeinu Tam that it occurs 72 minutes after sunset, the time it takes to walk 4 mil at 18 minutes per mil.

Calculated as 72 standard clock minutes after sunset, or after sea level sunset depending on the elevation setting.

According to the Machtzis Hashekel in Orach Chaim 235:3, the Pri Megadim in Orach Chaim 261:2, and others, the 72 minutes are standard clock minutes at any time of year in any location.

This time may not be available where the calculation cannot be computed, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

items.title: Tzais Geonim 3 Point 7 Degrees
items.text:
This zman is deprecated.

Tzais, or nightfall, according to an opinion of the Geonim, set when the sun is 3.7° below the geometric zenith. This corresponds to 13.5 minutes after sunset in Jerusalem around the equinox / equilux.

It is based on ¾ of a mil at 18 minutes per mil, which is 13.5 minutes after sunset; at that point the sun is 3.7° below the geometric zenith in Jerusalem around the equinox / equilux.

This calculation does not include the 26.46 seconds for walking 49 amos, described as the heref ayin of bain hashmashos of Rav Yosi, at the pace of an 18-minute mil.

Rabbi Yedidya Manet in Zmanei HaHalacha Lema'aseh, 4th edition part 2, pages 22 and 24, lists 3.65°, which is described as appearing to be a drop too early.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTzaisGeonim3Point7Degrees

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTzaisGeonim3Point7Degrees

items.title: Tzais Geonim 3 Point 8 Degrees
items.text:
This zman is deprecated.

Tzais, or nightfall, according to an opinion of the Geonim, set when the sun is 3.8° below the geometric zenith. This corresponds to 14 minutes after sunset in Jerusalem around the equinox / equilux.

It is based on ¾ of a mil at 18 minutes per mil, or 13.5 minutes after sunset, plus 30 seconds for the time to walk 49 amos, described as the heref ayin of bain hashmashos of Rav Yosi. This totals 14 minutes after sunset; at that point the sun is 3.8° below the geometric zenith in Jerusalem around the equinox / equilux.

At the pace of an 18-minute mil, 49 amos would take 26.46 seconds, rounded to 30 seconds.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTzaisGeonim3Point8Degrees

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTzaisGeonim3Point8Degrees

items.text:

Tzais, or nightfall, according to an opinion of the Geonim, set when the sun is 4.42° below the western horizon. It is described as a very early zman.

It is based on ¾ of a mil using a 22.5-minute mil, which is 16 ⅞ minutes after sunset.

This very early zman should not be relied on without Rabbinical guidance.

This calculation does not include the 33.07 seconds for walking 49 amos, described as the heref ayin of bain hashmashos of Rav Yosi, at the pace of a 22.5-minute mil.

Rabbi Yedidya Manet in Zmanei HaHalacha Lema'aseh, 4th edition part 2, pages 22 and 24, lists 4.37°, which is described as appearing to be a drop too early.

items.text:
This zman is deprecated.

Tzais, or nightfall, according to an opinion of the Geonim, set when the sun is 4.66° below the western horizon. It is described as a very early zman.

It is based on ¾ of a mil using a 24-minute mil, which is 18 minutes after sunset.

This very early zman should not be relied on without Rabbinical guidance.

This calculation does not include the 35.28 seconds for walking 49 amos, described as the heref ayin of bain hashmashos of Rav Yosi, at the pace of a 24-minute mil.

items.title: Tzais Geonim 4 Point 8 Degrees
items.text:
Tzais, or nightfall, according to an opinion of the Geonim, set when the sun is 4.8° below the western horizon. It is described as an early zman for tzais.

It is based on ¾ of a 24-minute mil, plus 0.6 minutes for the time to walk 49 amos for bain hashmashos of Rav Yosi, for a total of 18.6 minutes after sunset. This corresponds to the sun being 4.8° below the western horizon.

This is based on Rav Yechiel Michel Shlezinger's sefer Aizehu Bain Hashmashos and Rabbi Yehuda (Leo) Levi's calculations in Zmanei Hayom BaHalacha, p. 37.

At this point, 3 medium-sized stars are visible to a non-expert with good vision with effort. An expert who knows where to look can see 3 medium stars as early as 15 minutes after sunset.

Hazmanim Bahalacha is cited as explaining that medium-sized stars would be visible to an expert as early as 14 minutes after sunset, based on 13.5 minutes for ¾ of an 18-minute mil plus 0.5 minutes for bain hashmashos of Rav Yosi.

This early zman for tzais should not be relied on without Rabbinical guidance.

In northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, this time may not be available when the sun does not reach low enough below the horizon.

Technical details
