Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTzaisGeonim4Point8Degrees

items.title: Tzais Geonim 5 Point 95 Degrees
items.text:
Tzais, or nightfall, according to an opinion of the Geonim, set when the sun is 5.95° below the geometric zenith. This is tied to the sun’s position 24 minutes after sunset in Jerusalem around the equinox / equilux.

The 24 minutes are based on the Baal Hatanya’s calculation of 18 minutes, which is ¾ of a 24-minute mil, plus 4 minutes for shkiah amitis, plus 2 minutes for bain hashmashos of Rav Yosi.

Sources cited include Hazmanim Bahalacha vol. II, ch. 50 no. 5, pp. 512–513, ch. 47, and Yisrael Vehazmanim Vol III, ch. 13, no. 53, p. 1026.

Among the sources mentioned for this zman are Rabbi Yehuda (Leo) Levi's calculations in Jewish Chrononomy and other sources.

Calculations are described as showing that the time is closer to 5.93° and was seemingly rounded to 5.95°.

Chabad calendars usually use a 6°-based time built on this same calculation.

Rabbi Yedidya Manet in Zmanei HaHalacha Lema'aseh, 4th edition part 2, pages 22 and 24, lists 5.88°, which is described as appearing to be a drop too early.

In northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, this time may not be available when the sun does not reach low enough below the horizon.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTzaisGeonim5Point95Degrees

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTzaisGeonim5Point95Degrees

items.title: Tzais Geonim 6 Point 45 Degrees
items.text:
Tzais, or nightfall, according to the opinion of the Geonim as calculated by Rabbi Yechiel Michel Tucazinsky, set when the sun is 6.45° below the geometric zenith. It is described as the commonly used zman in Israel.

It is based on the sun’s position no later than 31 minutes after sea-level sunset in Jerusalem. At the summer solstice, calculations show that 30.75 minutes after shkiah corresponds to 6.45° below the geometric zenith. At the equinox, 6.45° is described as 26.5 minutes after shkiah around the equinox / equilux.

The Birur Halacha is cited as showing that Rav Tucazinsky’s sunset calculations, listed as 28 minutes in this case, were about 3 minutes later than reality.

This calculation is found in the Birur Halacha Yoreh Deah 262.

It is also used in the Luach Itim Lebinah.

This differs from the 6.1° / 6.2° calculation for Rabbi Tucazinsky’s time as calculated by Hazmanim Bahalacha vol. II, chapter 50:7, p. 515.

In northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, this time may not be available when the sun does not reach low enough below the horizon.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTzaisGeonim6Point45Degrees

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTzaisGeonim6Point45Degrees

items.title: Tzais Geonim 7 Point 083 Degrees
items.text:
Tzais, or nightfall, according to an opinion of the Geonim, set when the sun is 7.083°, also written as 7° 5′, below the western horizon.

It is based on Dr. Baruch Berthold Cohn’s observation of 3 medium-sized stars in his luach Tabellen enthaltend die Zeitangaben für den Beginn der Nacht und des Tages für die Breitengrade + 66 bis -38, published in Strasbourg in 1899.

This calendar was very popular in Europe, and many other calendars based their tzais time on it.

Rav Dovid Tzvi Hoffman, in שו״ת מלמד להועיל, in an exchange of letters with Baruch Cohn in Orach Chaim 30, agreed to this zman, as did the שו״ת בני ציון and the תנובת שדה.

It is very close to the time of the מקור חסד of the Sefer chasidim.

It is close to, but not exactly, the sun’s position 30 minutes after sunset in Jerusalem around the equinox / equilux. The actual position of the sun 30 minutes after sunset in Jerusalem is stated as 7.205° at the equilux and 7.199° at the equinox.

Hazmanim Bahalacha vol. 2, pages 520–521 is cited for more details.

In northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, this time may not be available when the sun does not reach low enough below the horizon.

Technical details

Source method: ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTzaisGeonim7Point083Degrees

Technical reference: com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getTzaisGeonim7Point083Degrees

items.title: Tzais Geonim 7 Point 67 Degrees
items.text:
Tzais, or nightfall, according to the Geonim, based on 45 minutes after sunset during the summer solstice in New York, when twilight is longest. Sources cited for this zman include Igros Moshe Even Haezer 4, Ch. 4, Rabbi Heber's Shaarei Zmanim, chapter 10 (page 87), chapter 12 (page 108), Rabbi Simcha Bunim Cohen's The radiance of Shabbos, Divrei Shalom Vol. III, chapter 75, and Bais Av"i Vol. III, chapter 117.

Calculated as the time when the sun is 7.67° below sea level, corresponding to the sun’s position 45 minutes after sunset during the summer solstice in New York.

This zman depends on the level of light; Rabbi Yaakov Shakow presented this degree-based calculation to Rabbi Shmuel Kamenetsky, who agreed to it.

The time may not be available in northern or southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle or north of the Antarctic Circle, where the sun may not go low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

items.text:

items.title: Tzais Geonim 8 Point 5 Degrees

Tzais, or nightfall, when the sun is 8.5° below the geometric horizon after sunset. Rabbi Meir Posen in the Ohr Meir calculated this as the time when 3 small stars are visible, which is later than the required 3 medium stars.

Calculated as the time when the sun is 8.5° below the geometric zenith. This is based on the sun’s position below the horizon 36 minutes after sunset in Jerusalem around the equinox / equilux.

The time may not be available in northern or southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle or north of the Antarctic Circle, where the sun may not go low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

Technical details

Source method: ZmanimCalendar.getTzaisGeonim8Point5Degrees
